coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical oxidation of seawater using vanadium facilitated quaternary layered double hydroxides integrated with sulfur-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Vedanarayanan, Mahalakshmi
Pitchai, Chandrasekaran
Chen, Chih-Ming
Gopalakrishnan, Sethuraman Mathur
coredata.dc:description: This research introduces a novel approach to electrocatalysis for sustainable energy generation, revealing the MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs composite supported by nickel foam (NF) as a high-performance catalyst specifically designed for seawater electrolysis. Constructed by incorporating sulfur-doped carbon dots (SCDs) into MnCoCrV layered double hydroxide (LDH) and depositing them onto a nickel foam substrate, this electrocatalyst demonstrates exceptional efficiency in the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) under alkaline seawater conditions. MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF attains a noteworthy current density of 10 mA/cm² with a minimal overpotential of 209.4 mV. Additionally, it demonstrates a reduced Tafel value of 81.5 mV/dec, indicating faster kinetics. The electrode maintains impressive long-term stability, sustaining efficiency for approximately 50 h at a constant current density of 10 mA/cm². The increased surface area and reduced charge transfer resistance contribute to substantial electrocatalytic performance in seawater. This performance is primarily attributed to improved conductivity, resulting from synergistic contributions from high-valence-state vanadium ions and electrochemically active functional groups in SCDs. The MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF electrocatalyst stands out for its intricate features that not only promote efficient electron transfer but also effectively counteract interference from chloride anions in seawater electrolysis. This study underscores the innovative nature of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF as a pivotal development in electrocatalyst research, offering a promising avenue for harnessing renewable energy from seawater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quaternary layered double hydroxides
Seawater oxidation
Sulphur-doped carbon dots
OER

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of MnCoCrV LDH and MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs
OER performance in alkaline freshwater and seawater electrolytes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Innovation and Development Center of Sustainable Agriculture

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Ms. Mahalakshmi Vedanarayanan expresses gratitude to the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC)-International Internship Pilot Program (IIPP) for providing the internship opportunity in Taiwan and for their financial support. This work was financially supported by the \u201CInnovation and Development Center of Sustainable Agriculture\u201D from The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in Taiwan.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of sulphur doped carbon dots (SCDs).
Synthesis of MnCoCrV LDH and MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs.
XRD Spectra of a) MnCoCrV LDH and MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs and b) SCDs.
SEM images of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs (a&b) and MnCoCrV LDH (c&d) and MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs coated nickel foam before electrolysis (e&f).
EDX mapping of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs coated nickel foam.
HR-TEM images of a) MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs at 2 nm scale and b) 20 nm scale and c) SCDs.
XPS spectra of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs (a) V 2p level, (b) Mn 2p level, (c) Co 2p level, (d) Cr 2p level, (e) C 1s level, (f) O 1s level, (g) S 2p level and (h) Survey spectra before and after OER.
BET analysis of a) MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs and b) MnCoCrV LDH.

(a) LSV curves for respective MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF, MnCoCrV LDH/NF, SCDs/NF, RuO2/NF and Bare NF in alkaline water and (b) comparative LSV curves and (c) overpotentials (Inset Figure) of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF in alkaline water, alkaline seawater and simulated seawater.

(a) Tafel slope values for respective MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF, MnCoCrV LDH/NF, SCDs/NF, RuO2/NF and Bare NF in alkaline water and (b) comparative Tafel values of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF in alkaline water, alkaline seawater and simulated seawater.

(a) Nyquist plots for respective MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF and MnCoCrV LDH/NF in alkaline water and (b) comparative Nyquist plots of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF in alkaline water, alkaline seawater and simulated seawater.

Cyclic voltammetry curves of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF in (a) alkaline water, (b) simulated seawater and (c) alkaline seawater and (d) Linear fits of current density vs. scan rate in all the three electrolytes.
Chronopotentiometry studies for MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF (a) in 1 M KOH (b) in 1 M KOH + seawater and (c) in 1 M KOH + 0.5 M NaCl (Inset Figure).
(a&b) SEM images of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF after electrolysis.
XPS spectra of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs (a) V 2p level, (b) Mn 2p level, (c) Co 2p level, (d) Cr 2p level, (e) C 1s level, (f) O 1s level and (g) S 2p level after OER.
Values from LSV polarization curves and Tafel slopes of the synthesized materials in alkaline medium.
Comparative table between the overpotentials of the synthesized MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs and the previously reported works in alkaline water, alkaline seawater and alkaline simulated seawater.
Comparative table for Cdl value of the synthesized MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs in alkaline seawater with the previously reported works.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical oxidation of seawater using vanadium facilitated quaternary layered double hydroxides integrated with sulfur-doped carbon dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research introduces a novel approach to electrocatalysis for sustainable energy generation, revealing the MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs composite supported by nickel foam (NF) as a high-performance catalyst specifically designed for seawater electrolysis. Constructed by incorporating sulfur-doped carbon dots (SCDs) into MnCoCrV layered double hydroxide (LDH) and depositing them onto a nickel foam substrate, this electrocatalyst demonstrates exceptional efficiency in the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) under alkaline seawater conditions. MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF attains a noteworthy current density of 10 mA/cm² with a minimal overpotential of 209.4 mV. Additionally, it demonstrates a reduced Tafel value of 81.5 mV/dec, indicating faster kinetics. The electrode maintains impressive long-term stability, sustaining efficiency for approximately 50 h at a constant current density of 10 mA/cm². The increased surface area and reduced charge transfer resistance contribute to substantial electrocatalytic performance in seawater. This performance is primarily attributed to improved conductivity, resulting from synergistic contributions from high-valence-state vanadium ions and electrochemically active functional groups in SCDs. The MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF electrocatalyst stands out for its intricate features that not only promote efficient electron transfer but also effectively counteract interference from chloride anions in seawater electrolysis. This study underscores the innovative nature of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF as a pivotal development in electrocatalyst research, offering a promising avenue for harnessing renewable energy from seawater.

ce:section.ce:para: In light of the world's expanding population and advancing economies, the call for renewable energy has never been more fascinating to fulfill the rushing global energy hunger. As the world strives to find more environmentally friendly and sustainable energy solutions, the electrochemical watersplitting reactions provide a window into a future where clean energy generation is not only highly efficient but also environmentally more responsible [1]. For water splitting, it is imperative to make use of our fresh water resources. To safeguard these freshwater reserves, seawater can be seen as a supplementary source, demonstrating a significant role in facilitating water splitting reactions [2]. Despite the abundant availability of coastal water, its processing proves challenging due to the high concentration of chloride (Cl−) anions [3]. These chloride ions can potentially obstruct the active sites of the catalyst employed in seawater electrolysis. Furthermore, adjusting the pH of seawater to become alkaline can lead to the generation of precipitates like Ca(OH)2, Mg(OH)2, and other insoluble solids, potentially diminishing the catalytic efficiency and overall longevity [4]. Consequently, it is essential to develop a novel catalyst capable of addressing the challenges caused by chloride ions and precipitates while also achieving superior efficiency during electrolysis when compared to current state-of-the-art materials, such as platinum-based catalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and RuO2 and IrO2 for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) [5]. In the pursuit of creating highly active and selective anodic electrodes for OER while effectively suppressing the chlorine evolution reaction (ClER) in seawater electrolysis, there has been a recent surge in attention towards transition metal layered double hydroxides (TM-LDHs) possessing two-dimensional (2D) structures [6]. Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) are 2D anionic lamellar compounds comprising brucite-like host layers consisting of a positive charge and charge-balancing (exchangeable) interlayer anions. Their general formula is expressed as [M(II)1-xM(III)x(OH)2(An)x/n.yH2O], where M(II) denotes a divalent cation such as Ni2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, and M(III) represents a trivalent metal cation like Al3+, Cr3+, Fe3+, and Mn3+. An stands for a non-framework charge compensating anion, which may be either inorganic or organic, including CO3 2−, NO3−, SO4 2−, and Cl− [7]. LDHs are regarded as exceptional catalysts for enhancing OER kinetics due to their hydroxyl-containing surface host layers.

In our prior research, ternary CoMnCr LDHs were synthesized for OER [8]. Nonetheless, Hu et al. have stressed the significance of incorporating vanadium into the LDH host layer, which notably enhanced OER performance [9]. Consequently, we synthesized quaternary LDH, termed manganese cobalt chromium vanadium layered double hydroxides (MnCoCrV LDH), using a urea hydrolysis method followed by a hydrothermal process. The addition of vanadium is anticipated to boost OER activity owing to its heightened electronic conductivity and enriched active sites. Furthermore, the presence of V4+ and V5+ ions, with their electron-withdrawing effect, may elevate the valence of adjacent 3d transition metals, thereby expediting the water oxidation reaction [9].

Many reports suggest that carbon allotropes such as carbon nanowires, carbon nanotubes, carbon dots, among others, serve as effective electron-conducting materials, hastening electron transport. Additionally, they contribute to increased surface area, significantly enhancing the stability of the catalyst structure [10]. Carbon dots (CDs) are particularly esteemed as excellent co-catalysts and electron carriers for electrochemical water-splitting reactions, showcasing remarkable electron transfer capabilities [11]. Moreover, they offer advantages such as cost-effectiveness, environmental friendliness, surface functionalization, improved electron transfer properties, electron reservoir capabilities, and high surface area [12,13]. Additionally, the integration of sulfur-doped carbon dots (SCDs) onto LDHs provides surface-rich functional groups such as OH, COOH, CS, and CO [14]. Ding et al. examined the electrocatalytic oxidation of alkaline water and seawater, employing hybrid materials comprising nitrogen-doped carbon dots paired with nickel iron LDH [15-17].

In this investigation, prawn shells, typically discarded as waste within the seafood industry, were repurposed for the synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon dots. This novel approach transforms waste material into a valuable resource, aligning with sustainability principles. Among the array of available techniques, hydrothermal methods emerge as promising avenues for fabricating highly pure and precisely defined nanostructures with diverse functional attributes. These methods offer notable advantages, including cost-effectiveness, high purity, and the ability to tailor the morphological characteristics of the resultant materials. Consequently, we employed the hydrothermal method to synthesize quaternary layered double hydroxides in conjunction with sulfur-doped carbon dots, denoted as MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs. This newly developed catalyst integrates multiple functionalities resulting from the incorporation of high-valence-state vanadium and high conductivity carbon dots, showing exceptional efficacy in seawater oxidation with a minimal overpotential. It is acknowledged as a prominent electrocatalyst for facilitating the oxygen evolution reaction in seawater.

Prawn shells sourced from the coastal region underwent thorough washing and were subsequently ground in a mixer using deionized water (DI water). Following grinding, the mixture was filtered to extract the shell components. Next, 56 mL of the shell extract was combined with 4 mL of diluted sulfuric acid and transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave was then subjected to hydrothermal carbonization at 180 °C for approximately 12 h in a hot air oven. Upon completion of the reaction, a brown-colored solution was obtained, which underwent centrifugation, filtration, and was designated as sulphur-doped carbon dots (SCDs) and the synthetic scheme was shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MnCoCrV LDH and MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Urea hydrolysis was initiated by dissolving 9 mmol each of Co(NO3)3·6H2O and Mn(NO3)3·4H2O, 3 mmol of Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, and 1 mmol of VCl3, along with 20 mmol of urea in 50 mL of DI water. The pH was adjusted to 10 using 1 M NaOH and 1 M Na2CO3. Subsequently, the precipitated solution underwent hydrothermal treatment at 200°C for 12 h. The resulting solution was thoroughly filtered with DI water followed by ethanol, centrifuged, and dried to yield manganese cobalt chromium vanadium layered double hydroxide (MnCoCrV LDH) as shown in Fig. 2 .

For the preparation of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs, a 2:1 ratio of SCDs to MnCoCrV LDH was taken and adjusted to pH 10 using 1 M NaOH and 1 M Na2CO3. The mixture was then subjected to hydrothermal treatment in an autoclave at 200 °C for 12 h. The resulting solution was filtered with DI water, followed by ethanol, centrifuged, and dried to yield manganese cobalt chromium vanadium layered double hydroxide supported with sulphur-doped carbon dots (MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs) as shown in Fig. 2.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of MnCoCrV LDH exhibited notable peaks at 11.5°, 23.8°, 34.6°, 60.5°, and 61.7°, corresponding to the crystallographic planes of (003), (006), (012), (110), and (113), respectively [18] as shown in Fig. 3 a (JCPDS no. 46–0605).

The presence of the (003) diffraction peak in both the XRD patterns of MnCoCrV LDH and MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs suggested the stacking of brucite-like layers within these structures. In contrast, the XRD pattern for the SCDs revealed a diffraction peak at 23.5°, indicating a graphitic structure associated with the (002) plane [19] as given in Fig. 3b (JCPDS no. 00–001–0478). The broadness of this peak was attributed to the small size of the SCDs. Moreover, the presence of the (002) plane in the XRD pattern of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs provided compelling evidence for the successful integration of SCDs into the MnCoCrV LDH structure, as evidenced by the shifts in the peaks of MnCoCrV LDH to 11.0°, 21.2°, 35.0°, 60.6°, and 62.4° in MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs, as illustrated in Fig. 3a.

Fig. 4 (a-d) presented SEM images revealing the formation of closely packed spherical architectures for both MnCoCrV LDH and MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs [20,21]. Before initiating the electrolysis process, Fig. 4 (e & f) illustrated the effective coating of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs in the depicted spherical morphology on nickel foam.

The appropriate distribution of elements, including manganese, cobalt, chromium, vanadium, sulfur, oxygen, and carbon within MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs on nickel foam, was verified through EDX mapping and EDX spectra, as depicted in Fig. 5 and Fig. S1, respectively. The composition of the synthesized MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs was given in Table S1. Additionally, Fig. S2 illustrated the distribution of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs elements within a single sphere, excluding nickel foam, to enhance clarity [22].

A thorough examination of the high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 6 a unveiled intricate details regarding the morphology of an individual sphere-like structure of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs. This structure, as defined by multiple nanorods aggregated to form a sphere [23], exhibited a spherical morphology on the surface as revealed by SEM analysis. Moreover, the lattice fringe values of 0.26 nm and 0.21 nm, corresponding to MnCoCrV LDH and SCDs, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 6a, indicated the integration of MnCoCrV LDH and SCDs into the structure [24]. To enhance clarity, additional images have been provided in the supplementary section, as depicted in Fig. S3 and Fig. S4.

The V 2p core-level spectrum in Fig. 7 a of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs featured peaks for V 2p3/2 and V 2p1/2. In the V 2p3/2 region, three components were detected at 515.8, 516.8, and 517.9 eV, corresponding to trivalent, tetravalent, and pentavalent vanadium ions, respectively. This suggested a partial oxidation of trivalent V ions to higher valence states during the synthesis of LDH [25,26]. The detection of Mn3+ within MnCoCr LDH@SCDs was confirmed by the presence of peaks at 641.4 eV for Mn 2p3/2 and 653.4 eV for Mn 2p1/2, as illustrated in Fig. 7b.

In the Co 2p spectrum (Fig. 7c), the appearance of two separate peaks at 781.0 eV and 796.9 eV indicated the presence of Co2+, while satellite peaks at 785.2 eV and 803.7 eV were linked to Co3+. In Fig. 7d, peaks at 577.2 and 577.9 eV for Cr 2p3/2 corresponded to Cr3+-O and Cr3+-OH, while the peak at 586.8 eV was attributed to Cr 2p1/2. The C 1 s spectrum analysis of the fresh sample, as shown in Fig. 7e, revealed three peaks at 284.6, 285.5, and 288.0 eV, representing CC/CO, CO, and OCO bonds, respectively. Furthermore, the O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 7f indicated the presence of M−O, M−OH, and M−water bonds at 531.2, 530.7, and 532.2 eV, respectively. The high-resolution S 2p XPS spectrum in Fig. 7g displayed two main peaks at 168.5 eV and 165.2 eV, indicating the existence of metal-S bonding [27].

UV–Visible spectroscopy was utilized to examine the optical characteristics of synthesized SCDs. In Figure S5, the UV–Visible spectrum exhibited two distinct shoulders at 270 and 330 nm, corresponding to π-π* and n-π* electronic transitions. These transitions provided confirmation of the presence of sp2 carbon (CC) and carbonyl groups in the carbon dots. The FT-IR spectrum of SCDs, as shown in Fig. S6, indicated the presence of functional groups including OH, CO, and CS at absorption bands of 3452, 1626, and 1125 cm−1, respectively. These observations indicate the formation of a surface abundant in oxygenous and sulfurous groups, which greatly aids in seawater electrolysis by promoting the evolution of oxygen moieties [28,29].

The analyses of the surface areas for MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs and MnCoCrV LDH were conducted using N2 adsorption-desorption curves. As illustrated in Fig. 8 a, the BET surface area for MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs was determined to be 136.4 m2/g, exceeding that of MnCoCrV LDH, which was measured at 126.3 m2/g (as shown in Fig. 8b). This increased specific surface area in MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs was attributed to the integration of SCDs into MnCoCrV LDH, contributing to superior catalytic activity by enhancing the density of active sites on the catalyst surface [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: OER performance in alkaline freshwater and seawater electrolytes
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic performance and resilience of the synthesized materials were evaluated using a conventional three-electrode arrangement. The assessment of their electrocatalytic efficiency involved testing with three different electrolytes: 1 M KOH alkaline solution, alkaline seawater, and simulated seawater electrolyte (1 M KOH + 0.5 M NaCl). In a 1 M KOH solution with DI water, MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF demonstrated superior OER activity compared to MnCoCrV LDH/NF, requiring only 188.1 mV for a current density of 10 mA/cm² (Fig. 9 a). This positions it among the top-performing OER catalysts in 1 M KOH, similar to RuO2/NF [31,32]. Conversely, other materials such as SCDs/NF and the bare NF electrode exhibited larger overpotentials, as detailed in Table 1 .

Following this, the OER performance of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF in 1 M KOH seawater, as well as in simulated seawater, was comprehensively evaluated, as depicted in Fig. 9b and c. MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF demonstrated superior OER efficiency, with a mere 209.4 mV overpotential required to achieve a current density of 10 mA/cm² in the alkaline seawater electrolyte. Conversely, the overpotential for simulated seawater was recorded at 196.9 mV. MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF surpassed many reported catalysts for seawater oxidation, as evidenced in Table 2 . Tafel slopes were calculated from data extracted from Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV) curves for various catalysts, including MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF, MnCoCrV LDH/NF, SCDs/NF, RuO2/NF, and the bare NF electrode. The significantly lower Tafel slope of 75.3 mV/dec, depicted in Fig. 10 a, emphasized the superior catalytic performance of the synthesized MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF electrocatalyst in 1 M KOH. In contrast, the Tafel slopes for the other catalysts were observed to be higher, as elaborated in Table 1.

In both the alkaline seawater electrolyte and simulated seawater electrolyte, MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF exhibited decreased Tafel slope values, measuring 81.5 mV/dec and 80.4 mV/dec, respectively (as shown in Fig. 10b). The improved OER kinetics arise from alterations in the electronic structure brought about by the active site metals. These changes are influenced by lattice distortion and defects, contributing to the attainment of efficient kinetics in seawater, despite its challenging composition [40,41].

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) provided valuable insights into the dynamics at the electrode/electrolyte interface, revealing a semicircular pattern in Fig. 11 that was modeled using an equivalent modified Randel's circuit for all catalysts. Noteworthy findings emerged from the Nyquist plot of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF in Fig. 11a, where a notably reduced charge transfer resistance was observed, measured at 22.3 Ω/cm2. This starkly contrasted with the recorded values for MnCoCrV LDH/NF (50.9 Ω/cm2) and the bare NF electrode (168.8 Ω/cm2). Interestingly, MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF exhibited significantly lower charge-transfer resistance (46.89 Ω/cm2) in alkaline seawater and in simulated seawater (38.25 Ω/cm2), indicative of accelerated charge transfer (Fig. 11b). This decreased charge transfer resistance aligns with enhanced OER performance, underscoring MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF's efficient charge transfer mechanisms and its exceptional OER activity in seawater [42-44].

To evaluate the effectiveness of the catalyst, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was utilized to measure the double-layer capacitance (Cdl), indicative of the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA). Linear fitting curves of current density at various scan rates obtained from the CV data are shown in Fig. 12 a-d. MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF displayed a Cdl of 0.0233 mF/cm², indicating a significantly larger ECSA of 0.5825 cm2. This suggests a higher exposure of active sites, both on the surface and within the active layers, highlighting the highly conductive spherical morphology consisting of numerous nanorods in MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF, compared to ECSA values of MnCoCrV LDH/NF (=0.4425 cm2) and SCDs/NF (=0.2350 cm2) as provided in Fig.S8. Conversely, in alkaline seawater and simulated seawater, MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF exhibited a Cdl of 0.0199 mF/cm² and 0.0173 mF/cm², resulting in final ECSA values of 0.4975 cm² and 0.4325 cm². The larger ECSA facilitated faster evolution, indicating improved kinetics in both seawater and simulated seawater [45,46]. In alkaline water, where chloride hindrance is absent, the ECSA of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF was notably higher. Consequently, to assess the superior ECSA value of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF in seawater electrolyte, it was compared with previously reported data, detailed in Table 3 .

Chronopotentiometry measurements were performed to evaluate the long-term durability using a three-electrode system in the respective electrolytes. Assessing electrocatalytic activity, MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF demonstrated exceptional stability over a 50-hour testing period at a constant 10 mA/cm² in 1 M KOH, as illustrated in Fig. 13 a. It exhibited minimal potential change of 0.0022 V (from 1.4196 VRHE to 1.4174 VRHE).

In contrast, when exposed to an alkaline seawater environment (ranging from 1.4397 VRHE to 1.4232 VRHE) and simulated seawater conditions (varying from 1.4268 VRHE to 1.4292 VRHE), MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF displayed marginal potential changes, as depicted in Fig. 13b and 13c. These results emphasize the significantly enhanced stability of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF in both alkaline seawater and simulated seawater [51,52]. Moreover, a stability test was conducted for approximately 50 h at a higher current density of 100 mA.cm−2 in alkaline seawater, revealing minimal potential change. This underscores the heightened stability of the material, as depicted in Fig. S9.

After undergoing electrolysis, the catalyst persisted on the nickel foam, demonstrating its structural stability and durability. Despite being subjected to electrolysis for approximately 50 h, the catalyst retained its spherical morphology on the surface of NF, indicating its ability to withstand prolonged use as shown in Fig. 14 . After OER, the vanadium peaks shifted to 516.4 eV and 523.6 eV, corresponding to V 2p3/2 and V 2p1/2, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 15 a. Additionally, oxidation to Mn4+ was observed, as indicated in Fig. 15b, where the peaks of Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 shifted to lower energy levels of 640.8 eV and 652.6 eV, respectively. In the Co 2p3/2 spectrum, two primary peaks at 780.1 and 796.0 eV were observed, assigned to Co(III) and Co(II), respectively, suggesting the oxidation of Co2+ to Co3+, as shown in Fig. 15c.

Following the OER durability test, it was observed that Cr 2p3/2 split into two peaks, with one at 576.2 eV attributed to Cr3+ and another at 586.0 eV attributed to Cr6+ (as shown in Fig. 15d). The C 1 s spectrum analysis of the fresh sample indicated four peaks at 284.1, 285.2, and 287.6 eV, corresponding to CC, CO, CO, and OCO bonds, respectively (as seen in Fig. 15e). Furthermore, the O 1s spectrum, shown in Fig. 15f, exhibited the presence of MO, MOH, and M−water bonds with binding energies of 530.7, 529.8, and 531.8 eV, respectively. The high-resolution S 2p XPS spectrum displayed two main peaks at 167.9 eV and 164.8 eV, respectively, indicating the existence of metal-S bonding in Fig. 15g. The survey spectra of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs before and after electrolysis were given in Fig. 7h, indicating the peak shifted to a lower energy level after electrolysis. This suggests the ability of electron transfer during OER due to the heightened basicity and robust basic sites in the LDH prove advantageous for augmenting OER activity. Additionally, the existence of chloride ions in electrolytes has the potential to enhance electrocatalytic activity for OER. This improvement may be attributed to the role of adsorbed chloride ions in stabilizing the higher valence state of the elements within the MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs during post-electrocatalysis, serving as active sites for the electrocatalytic OER. After undergoing electrolysis, the MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs on nickel foam was analyzed via XRD, indicating no additional peaks compared to the initial patterns. This implied that no new crystal phases emerged following 50 h of electrolysis as shown in Fig. S10 [34,53].

In summary, the synthesis of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs involved a successful process that included urea hydrolysis followed by hydrothermal treatment. While exhibiting commendable performance in alkaline freshwater, this material demonstrated exceptional effectiveness in alkaline seawater. MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF showcased outstanding catalytic activity, achieving a notable current density of 10 mA/cm² with a minimal overpotential of 209.4 mV, underscoring its impressive performance and stability. Moreover, it presented a lower Tafel value of 81.5 mV/dec, indicative of faster kinetics. The MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF electrode demonstrated remarkable long-term stability, maintaining its performance for approximately 50 h at a constant current density of 10 mA/cm². With an increased surface area and reduced charge transfer resistance, MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF displayed substantial electrocatalytic performance, primarily attributed to enhanced conductivity in seawater. The outstanding performance of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs can be attributed to high-valence-state vanadium ions in LDH and electron conductivity, along with negatively charged functional groups from sulfur-doped carbon dots (SCDs). Together, these characteristics not only facilitated electron transfer but also effectively repelled chlorine anions during seawater electrolysis. Consequently, the remarkable performance of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF positions it as a novel and promising electrocatalyst for the oxygen evolution reaction in alkaline seawater.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Ms. Mahalakshmi Vedanarayanan expresses gratitude to the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC)-International Internship Pilot Program (IIPP) for providing the internship opportunity in Taiwan and for their financial support. This work was financially supported by the “Innovation and Development Center of Sustainable Agriculture” from The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in Taiwan.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2024.144529.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supporting Information: Materials, Preparation of alkaline sea water and simulated seawater, Characterization of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs, MnCoCr LDHV and SCDs, and Electrochemical studies; EDX spectrum of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs (Fig. S1); Composition of elements in MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs (Table S1); EDX mapping of one single sphere of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs (Fig. S2); SEM images of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs(Fig. S3); HR-TEM images of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs (Fig. S4); UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. S5) and FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S6) of SCDS; Equivalent fitted circuit for bare NF (Fig. S7); Cyclic voltammetry curves and Linear fits of current density vs. scan rate of MnCoCrV LDH/NF and SCDs/NF in alkaline water (Fig. S8); Chronopotentiometry for MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs/NF in alkaline seawater for about 50 h at a constant current density of 100 mA.cm−2 (Fig. S9); XRD Spectrum of MnCoCrV LDH@SCDs after OER in alkaline seawater (Fig. S10).
